body:
### Describe the bug

The guard Cython emits around a user `__dealloc__` (`ModuleNode.generate_usr_dealloc_call`) marks the dying object as **live to other threads** on free-threaded builds, so a concurrent `PyUnstable_TryIncRef()` succeeds on an object already being deallocated → use-after-free (aborts with `Py_SET_REFCNT: Assertion 'refcnt >= 0' failed`).

```c
Py_SET_REFCNT(o, Py_REFCNT(o) + 1);   // guard
__pyx_pw_..._1__dealloc__(o);
Py_SET_REFCNT(o, Py_REFCNT(o) - 1);
```

At `tp_dealloc` the object is unowned/merged-zero (`ob_ref_shared == _Py_REF_MERGED`), where `TryIncRef` correctly refuses. But `Py_SET_REFCNT(o, 1)` on an unowned object takes the unowned branch — `ob_ref_shared = _Py_REF_SHARED(1, _Py_REF_MERGED) == 7` — i.e. "merged, **shared count 1**", indistinguishable from a live object, so `TryIncRef` now succeeds for the whole `__dealloc__`. The non-atomic unbump then clobbers the racing thread's increment.

```
                ob_ref_shared   TryIncRef
entry           3 (merged,0)    refused
after  +1 bump  7 (merged,1)    SUCCEEDS   <-- looks alive to other threads
racing TryIncRef 11 (merged,2)
after  -1 bump  3 (merged,0)    (clobbers the +4)  -> freed; other thread holds a dangling ref
```

`TryIncRef` is correct — a merged shared count of 1 is the legitimate encoding of a live unowned object; the bug is the guard writing it onto a dying one. Only `__dealloc__` is affected (`__del__`/`tp_finalize` has no guard).

### Code to reproduce the behaviour:

`cythonize -i bug.pyx` on `python3.14t`, then `python -c "import bug; bug.run()"` → aborts within a few thousand iterations on an unfixed build; `PYTHON_GIL=1` never faults.

```cython
# cython: freethreading_compatible=True
cdef extern from *:
    """
    #include <stdatomic.h>
    static _Atomic(PyObject*) slot;
    static atomic_long caught;
    static void publish(PyObject *o){ atomic_store(&slot, o); }
    static void enable(PyObject *o){ PyUnstable_EnableTryIncRef(o); }
    static void consume(int (*dying)(PyObject*)){
        PyObject *o = atomic_load(&slot);
        if (o && PyUnstable_TryIncRef(o)) {      /* borrowed -> strong upgrade */
            if (dying(o)) atomic_fetch_add(&caught, 1);
            Py_DECREF(o);
        }
    }
    static long caught_count(void){ return atomic_load(&caught); }
    """
    void publish(object)
    void enable(object)
    void consume(int (*)(object) noexcept)
    long caught_count()

from threading import Thread

cdef int is_dying(object o) noexcept:
    return (<D>o).dying

cdef class D:
    cdef public int dying
    def __cinit__(self):
        enable(self); publish(self)          # publish a borrowed pointer
    def __dealloc__(self):
        self.dying = 1                       # mark mid-deallocation
        cdef int i
        for i in range(200): pass

def _consume(long n):
    cdef long i
    for i in range(n): consume(is_dying)

def run(long producer=100000, long consumer=2000000):
    t = Thread(target=_consume, args=(consumer,)); t.start()
    for _ in range(producer):
        d = D(); d = None
    t.join()
    print("TryIncRef hit an in-__dealloc__ object", caught_count(), "times")
```

### Expected behaviour

The guard should keep the object alive without making it acquirable by other threads — as CPython's own `_PyObject_ResurrectStart/End` do, by re-taking ownership and leaving the *shared* refcount at the refuse sentinel:

```c
op->ob_tid = _Py_ThreadId(); op->ob_ref_local = 1; op->ob_ref_shared = 0;  // TryIncRef refused
```

The recursion guard still holds (owner-thread inc/dec moves `ob_ref_local`). These stores need no `Py_BUILD_CORE` — available in the `CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON_FREETHREADING` config. Patch + test to follow as a PR.

### OS

Linux (any free-threaded build)

### Python version

3.14t / 3.13t (`Py_GIL_DISABLED`)

### Cython version

master (3.3.0a1) and 3.2.x

### Additional context

Distinct from #7391 (Limited-API symbol availability on the same lines) and #7115; neither covers this race.

comment_data.body: It's a future problem, but that seems unsolveable in the abi3t stable ABI?

@da-woods Thanks for fast responding! Yes, that is still unsolveable in the abi3t stable ABI(per PEP 803). But our downstream project is now kind of blocked by the false positive results from `PyUnstable_TryIncRef`. A temporary patch would be appreciated for 3.14t at the moment. We are looking forward to your comments about this problem, as the `ModuleNode.generate_usr_dealloc_call` may be refactored to adapt the future abi3t I guess?
Yes - nothing done now needs to accommodate ABI3t so if you have a patch then please submit it.
@da-woods Thanks for classification! I have created a patch pr #7770. Any comments and suggestions are appreciated:)

> that seems unsolveable in the abi3t stable ABI

Py3.15(t) is already in beta, so this is worth reporting to CPython.
@scoder Agree. It would be perfect if CPython officially addresses it by semantic.

I've reported it [to CPython here](https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/152406).

I think it's possible to solve this in the Py3.15 Limited API by throwing everything with `__dealloc__` into a "to be cleaned up later" list and tying that to the GC being called.
I've got a quick proof-of-concept at https://github.com/da-woods/cython/commit/f62edea8a23980fe706ae815a84a8a9a7b3a8aff but it needs a bit of cleanup and probably a bit more work to convince myself that it's actually safe. It also doesn't feel like a great workaround.

I understand your concerns. I've seen code trying very hard to avoid reference cycles, and `__dealloc__` is a very common method to have when it comes to wrapping C libraries. Intransparently postponing the cleanup is really not something we should do, if we can avoid it at all. It might easily keep users away from the Stable ABI, or let them run into the trap of innocently introducing subtle memory issues, which might be even more pressing in a parallel thread processing setup.

Let's give the CPython devs a moment to discuss this and see what comes out of it.

Yes -  there's no reason to merge anything like what I've proposed until we support the freethreading limited API anyway so it's not an urgent problem. I'm just exploring it a bit to try to work out if it's workable.

For regular builds we can definitely just use the "copy the internals" fix.
Thanks @da-woods and @scoder for helping resolve this issue in such a short time!

title: [BUG] `__dealloc__` refcount guard can resurrect a dying object on free-threaded builds
